key_points: Time measurement in early civilizations was based on the regular motions of celestial bodies
The Earth's rotation on its axis causes the cycle of day and night
The Earth's revolution around the Sun determines the length of a year
The Moon's revolution around the Earth causes the repeating pattern of lunar phases
Constellations appear to move across the sky due to Earth's rotation
Different constellations become visible in different seasons because of Earth's revolution around the Sun
The North Star appears almost fixed because it lies nearly along Earth's rotational axis
examples: Sundials used the shadow of a stick to estimate time based on the Sun’s position
The lunar calendar uses the Moon's cycle of about 29.5 days to define a month
Ancient sailors used constellations such as Ursa Major to navigate at night
Seasonal changes in visible constellations helped early farmers plan agricultural activities
formulas: 1 day = Time taken by Earth to complete one rotation (≈ 24 hours)
1 year = Time taken by Earth to complete one revolution around the Sun (≈ 365 days)
1 lunar month ≈ 29.5 days (one full cycle of Moon phases)
exam_tips: Clearly explain the difference between Earth's rotation and revolution
Understand how celestial movements help measure time periods like day, month, and year
Use examples such as sundials and lunar calendars when explaining early time measurement
Remember how constellations and the North Star were used for navigation and time tracking
